---
pagetitle: "Chapter 2: An Introduction to R — Slides"
engine: knitr
format:
  live-revealjs:
    theme: simple
    transition: slide
    slide-number: true
    toc: false
    chalkboard: true
    footer: "STA258 | Chapter 2"
    pdf-separate-fragments: false
    html-math-method: mathjax
    code-copy: true
    code-fold: false
    webr:
      packages:
        - ggplot2
    live:
      show-solutions: true
---

# Chapter 2: An Introduction to R {.title-slide}

<style>
.reveal {
  font-family: Arial, sans-serif;
  color: #2f3437;
}

.reveal h1,
.reveal h2,
.reveal h3 {
  color: #2f3437;
}

.reveal h2 {
  border-bottom: 3px solid #00695c;
  padding-bottom: 4px;
  margin-bottom: 12px;
  font-size: 1.6em;
}

.title-slide {
  text-align: center;
}

.reveal p, .reveal li {
  font-size: 0.78em;
  line-height: 1.35;
}

.defbox {
  background: #c8e6f3;
  padding: 10px 14px;
  border-left: 6px solid #00695c;
  border-radius: 8px;
  font-size: 0.72em;
  margin-bottom: 8px;
  line-height: 1.35;
}

.examplebox {
  background: #eeeeee;
  padding: 10px 14px;
  border-radius: 8px;
  font-size: 0.72em;
  margin-bottom: 8px;
  line-height: 1.35;
}

.thinkbox {
  background: #fff8e1;
  padding: 10px 14px;
  border-left: 6px solid #f9a825;
  border-radius: 8px;
  font-size: 0.72em;
  margin-bottom: 8px;
  line-height: 1.35;
}

.warnbox {
  background: #ffebee;
  padding: 10px 14px;
  border-left: 6px solid #c62828;
  border-radius: 8px;
  font-size: 0.72em;
  margin-bottom: 8px;
  line-height: 1.35;
}

.big {
  font-size: 1.05em;
  font-weight: bold;
  color: #00695c;
}

.footer-note {
  font-size: 0.65em;
  color: #666666;
}

.center {
  text-align: center;
}

.small-text {
  font-size: 0.72em;
}

.reveal table {
  font-size: 0.78em;
}

.defbox p, .examplebox p, .thinkbox p, .warnbox p,
.defbox li, .examplebox li, .thinkbox li, .warnbox li {
  font-size: 1em;
  margin-bottom: 4px;
}
</style>

{{< include ./_extensions/r-wasm/live/_knitr.qmd >}}

STA258: Statistics with Applied Probability
University of Toronto Mississauga

![](cat.png){width="300"}

::: notes
This chapter introduces R, RStudio, and the first programming ideas needed for the course.
:::

---

## Why Learn R? {#why-r .scrollable}

<div class="center">
<span class="big">Every statistician on Earth uses code.<br>You're about to learn why.</span>
</div>

. . .

::: {.examplebox}
**Three reasons:**

- **Reproducibility** — every step is written down. Re-run it next year and get the same answer.
- **Scale** — the same script that analyzes 10 rows also analyzes 10 million.
- **Sharing** — send your code, not screenshots of your clicks.
:::

. . .

::: {.thinkbox}
This whole chapter is just enough R to do the rest of STA258. You don't need to become a software engineer.
:::

---

## Roadmap {#roadmap .scrollable}

#### 2.1 The Statistical Computing Language
- What R is and why we use it
- Point-and-click vs code

#### 2.2 Installing R and RStudio
- R vs RStudio
- Installing R
- Installing RStudio
- The RStudio panes and workflow
- Positron

::: {.footer-note}
Sections 2.3 onward are coming soon.
:::

---

## 2.1 The Statistical Computing Language {#statistical-computing-language .scrollable}

<div class="defbox">
<strong>Definition — R</strong><br>
R is a programming language designed for data manipulation, statistics, graphics, and reproducible analysis.
</div>

::: {.examplebox}
**R is good at:**

- arithmetic on numbers, vectors, matrices
- producing publication-quality graphs
- using built-in and user-contributed packages
- saving entire analyses as scripts that anyone can re-run
:::

::: notes
Emphasize that R is free, open-source, and the standard tool in statistics.
:::

---

## Point-and-click vs Code {#point-vs-code .scrollable}

::::: columns
::: {.column width="50%"}
<div class="examplebox">
<strong>Point-and-click software</strong><br><br>
Quick at first. But every click is invisible — hard to repeat, hard to audit, hard to share.
</div>
:::

::: {.column width="50%"}
<div class="defbox">
<strong>R scripts</strong><br><br>
Every step is written down. The script <em>is</em> the analysis — you can re-run it, version it, share it, or modify one line and re-run everything.
</div>
:::
:::::

. . .

<div class="thinkbox">
<strong>Bottom line:</strong> R makes statistical work reproducible, transparent, and automatic.
</div>

---

## 2.2 R vs RStudio {#r-vs-rstudio .scrollable}

::::: columns
::: {.column width="50%"}
<div class="defbox">
<strong>R</strong><br>
The programming language. The thing that does the calculations.
</div>
:::

::: {.column width="50%"}
<div class="defbox">
<strong>RStudio</strong><br>
The interface. Where you write code, see plots, view files, and read output.
</div>
:::
:::::

. . .

::: {.examplebox}
**Analogy:** R is the **engine**. RStudio is the **dashboard**.<br>
You can drive an engine without a dashboard — but you wouldn't want to.
:::

. . .

<div class="warnbox">
<strong>Install order matters:</strong> install <strong>R first</strong>, then RStudio on top.
</div>

---

## 2.2.1 Installing R {#install-r .scrollable}

<div class="defbox">
Go to <code>cran.r-project.org</code> and pick your operating system.
</div>

::: {.examplebox}
- **Windows** → Download R for Windows → `base` → run the installer
- **macOS** → Download R for macOS → pick the matching `.pkg` file
- **Linux** → follow your distribution's instructions
:::

. . .

<div class="thinkbox">
Once installed, R can run from a terminal — but in this course we always use it through RStudio.
</div>

---

## 2.2.2 Installing RStudio {#install-rstudio .scrollable}

<div class="defbox">
<strong>RStudio</strong><br>
An integrated development environment (IDE) designed for working with R.
</div>

::: {.examplebox}
**RStudio gives you:**

- a script editor (write code)
- a console (run code)
- environment & history (see your objects)
- files / plots / packages / help (everything else)
:::

::: notes
Mention: RStudio is now made by Posit. Same product, new company name.
:::

---

## The Four RStudio Panes {#rstudio-panes .scrollable}

| Pane | What it does |
|:---|:---|
| **Source** | Write and edit `.R` scripts and `.qmd` files |
| **Console** | Run commands directly and see output |
| **Environment / History** | Inspect saved objects and past commands |
| **Files / Plots / Packages / Help** | Browse files, view graphs, manage packages |

. . .

<div class="thinkbox">
<strong>Shortcut:</strong> <code>Cmd + Enter</code> (Mac) or <code>Ctrl + Enter</code> (Windows) runs the current line.
</div>

---

## A Typical Workflow {#workflow .scrollable}

::: {.examplebox}
1. Open RStudio
2. Create a new R script (File → New File → R Script)
3. Type your code in the **Source** pane
4. Hit `Cmd/Ctrl + Enter` to run the current line
5. See results in the **Console** or **Plots** pane
6. Save the script so you can re-run later
:::

. . .

<div class="thinkbox">
Saving the script is the whole point — your analysis lives in the file, not in the console.
</div>

---

## 2.2.3 Positron {#positron .scrollable}

<div class="defbox">
<strong>Positron</strong><br>
Another desktop environment from Posit, designed for modern data science.
</div>

. . .

::: {.examplebox}
**For STA258:** you only need RStudio. Positron is worth knowing about, but not required.
:::

---

## Work in Progress
